multi_sensory.anchor_matched: Two senses are nominally engaged but at least one is weak or marginal

evidence.quote: They are all playing with the balloons, throwing them in the air and catching them. The adult is also interacting with the children and the balloons.
evidence.reasoning: Children are running, throwing, and catching balloons, providing clear proprioceptive and whole-body movement engagement.

evidence.quote: There are six white balloons and three yellow balloons scattered on the floor.
evidence.reasoning: Two balloon colors (white and yellow) provide some visual variety, though the material palette is limited to a single material type.

evidence.quote: The children are still playing with the balloons, some are throwing them against the black board on the right side of the room.
evidence.indicator: Tactile invitations (textures, materials to touch, wet/dry/soft/rough)
evidence.reasoning: Balloons offer a tactile experience (smooth, light, slightly elastic surface) when held and batted, though this is a single uniform material with limited tactile variety.

evidence.quote: The children are moving around the room, holding their balloons. The adult is walking among them, still talking and guiding their play.
evidence.reasoning: Free movement across the open floor space with balloons as props meaningfully engages proprioception, but no music is audible and no additional sensory materials are present, leaving the multi-sensory design incomplete.

multi_sensory.scorer_notes: The activity notes describe this as a 'balloon dance' with music, but no music is audible or visible in the recording. Proprioceptive engagement (movement, throwing, catching) is strong and clearly present. Tactile engagement via balloons is present but limited to a single uniform material. Visual engagement is minimal (two colors, one material). No auditory elements (music, instruments) are confirmed in the evidence. The setup engages two senses meaningfully (movement/proprioceptive and tactile/visual) but the tactile and visual dimensions are weak — all one material type — placing this at 0.5.

evidence.reasoning: Children move freely into the room and immediately engage with the balloons, suggesting the room walls provide an implicit boundary, but no dedicated zone marker makes the activity area self-evident beyond the room itself.

evidence.indicator: The boundary is intuitive to a child without verbal explanation from an adult

evidence.quote: The children are still playing with the balloons, some are throwing them against the black board on the right side of the room.

evidence.reasoning: The room's walls (including the blackboard) act as de facto boundaries, but these are architectural features of the room rather than intentional activity-zone markers, making the boundary implied rather than clearly designed.

boundary.scorer_notes: This is a whole-room movement activity (balloon dance), so the room itself functions as the boundary container. The walls and floor provide an implied zone, but there is no mat, tape, rug, or other intentional zone marker. The activity design is inherently open-space, which partially justifies the room-as-boundary interpretation, but the lack of any explicit zone marker and the adult's ongoing verbal guidance keep this at 0.5.

The space is entirely clear of furniture and clutter, balloons are on the floor at child reach, children enter and immediately engage without any obstruction, and the open floor naturally invites the standing/moving posture the activity requires. clean_up.scorer_notes: The session ends at 04:50-04:59 with all children and the adult exiting the room, leaving balloons scattered on the floor. No cleanup sequence is observed at any point in the recording. The activity is a free-movement balloon dance in an open room with no visible storage infrastructure (no shelves, bins, labels, or designated return locations visible). Because cleanup never occurs and no storage design is observable, there is insufficient evidence to score this dimension.
